CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent monsoon moisture during the month of August resulted in above normal precipitation for much of western Colorado. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas, particularly down in southwest Colorado. Elsewhere, conditions remained unchanged through the end of the month. The only area of D3 (extreme) drought was the far southwest portion of the Four Corners / Upper Dolores River Basin zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved slightly during the month of August. The extreme (D3) drought over the western half of the zone was trimmed back to only include the far southwest portion. Elsewhere, severe (D2) conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 10 miles south of Whitewater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Dominguez Fire started in late August from a lightning strike, 10 miles south of Whitewater, and grew to 250 acres before it was contained in early November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level wind gradient tightened ahead of and with the passage of a cold front. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 71 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Craig Moffat County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level wind gradient tightened ahead of and with the passage of a cold front. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level wind gradient tightened ahead of and with the passage of a cold front. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station located 1 mile north-northwest of Glenwood Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front and strong 160 knot jet resulted in snow showers for the mountains of western Colorado. Amounts were generally light to moderate, though strong winds near 60 mph resulted in considerable blowing snow and reduced visibility for some ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 mph which resulted in considerable blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a cold front which led to strong radiational cooling across western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the Grand Valley where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 28 to 32 degrees across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong 160 knot upper level jet associated with an approaching system dug into the Great Basin. As it approached western Colorado it resulted in a tightening wind gradient with gusty winds at the surface. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Rangely Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, an isolated thunderstorm developed over Northeast Colorado moving southeast across Yuma County. The storm reached it's peak strength in the southeast corner of the county near the town of Idalia, Colorado where it produced nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jagged nickel sized hail reported as the storm moved over the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved over Eastern Colorado.  A corridor of high winds from the upper level disturbance reached the ground over Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  The highest reported wind gust was 61 MPH in Kit Carson County.  The high winds led to dust storm conditions near Aroya, causing Highway 40 to be closed and blew a semi over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high winds caused brown out conditions in the blowing dust at Aroya.  This, along with an overturned semi, led to Highway 40 being closed at the intersection of Highway 94 outside of Aroya.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed little from the previous day of high winds and blowing dust.  The upper level disturbance continued to slide northeast over the Upper Midwest.  High winds rounding the backside of the disturbance were able to reach the ground over East Central Colorado.  The peak gust was 66 MPH at the Burlington airport.  Dust storms also occurred due to the high winds and dry ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 66 MPH were reported across the county.  The highest gust reported was at the Burlington airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, deep, and cold upper level low remained situated over eastern Idaho and western Wyoming. Ample moisture associated with this system paired with modest instability from clearing skies resulted in the development of some stronger thunderstorms across western Colorado. Ping pong ball sized hail fell with a storm in Montrose County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail fell in Nucla in Montrose County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across western Colorado. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Severe wind gusts up to 61 mph occurred in Garfield County. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding and debris flows across portions of San Miguel, Montrose, and Mesa counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared in the wake of a departing upper level low. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the Upper Yampa River Basin where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 29 to 32 degrees across the area, including a low of 32 degrees at the Bob Adams Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies underneath a strong ridge of high pressure allowed temperatures to plummet across portions of western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures across numerous lower valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 22 to 32 degrees across the area. This was a season-ending freeze for the Lower Yampa River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies underneath a strong ridge of high pressure allowed temperatures to plummet across portions of western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures across numerous lower valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 25 to 30 degrees across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Eagle County Regional Airport. This was a season-ending freeze for the Central Colorado River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies underneath a strong ridge of high pressure allowed temperatures to plummet across portions of western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures across numerous lower valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 27 to 32 degrees across the area, including a low of 30 degrees at the Durango La Plata County Airport. This was a season-ending freeze for the Animas River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the western United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of western Colorado. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys with gusts of 60 mph or more in the mountains. This led to widespread blowing snow in the higher elevations which, when paired with moderate to heavy snow, resulted in near blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 12 inches estimated at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area at elevations above 10,000 feet. Lesser amounts of 4 to 7 inches fell at the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status remained unchanged for October, with the northeast part of the county in D2 Severe and D3 Extreme Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The precipitation for the month ranged from a hundredth of an inch to one and a half inches, with the highest amounts occurring over the west half of the county.  This was two inches below normal to near normal.  Normal precipitation ranges from one to one and a half inches.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of high winds were observed across much of southern Colorado during this time frame. The first part of this period was due to a mountain wave over the foothills of the eastern mountains, with a more widespread high wind event then occurring over the southern San Luis Valley and the eastern plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Eastern Colorado.  Ahead of the front southwest winds gusted up to 64 MPH, with the highest reported gust occurring at Burlington.  High winds from the southwest are rather unusual given that the vast majority of high wind events occur with northwest winds.  There was also blowing dust reported with the high winds, which caused visibilities to be reduced to less than two miles at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported near Firstview.

The first part of this period was due to a mountain wave over the foothills of the eastern mountains, with a more widespread high wind event then occurring over the southern San Luis Valley and the eastern plains.

Isolated to scattered thunderstorms spread across the region on this day. The overall severe threat was low, however, a few stronger storms produced severe wind gusts up to 60 to 65 mph across the southern I-25 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies continued to clear in the wake of a departing upper level low. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the Upper Yampa River Basin where vegetation was susceptible to damage. This was a season-ending freeze for the Upper Yampa River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 26 to 30 degrees across the area. This was a season-ending freeze for the Upper Yampa River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies underneath a strong ridge of high pressure allowed temperatures to plummet across portions of northwest Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the Central Yampa and Central Colorado River Basins where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from the mid to upper 20s across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Eagle County Regional Airport. This was a season-ending freeze for the Central Colorado River Basin.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds occurred out ahead of and along an unseasonably strong cold front that passed across western Colorado. Widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred. Some stronger winds occurred as the result of decaying outflow boundaries. This led to locally higher winds stronger than 58 mph in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin. These locally stronger winds led to some damage outside of Montrose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Nucla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern with a fair amount of moisture and instability supported several waves of showers and thunderstorms over much  of southern Colorado. A few stronger storms did occur, providing isolated heavy rainfall amounts of just over one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.07 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed in the mountains and foothills during the morning, then across the far northeast plains late in the morning into early afternoon.  In the mountains and foothills, peak wind gusts included:  91 mph at Berthoud Pass, 81 mph, 3 miles south-southwest of Rocky Flats; 77 mph, 8 miles southwest of Tabernash; and 76 mph at the junction of highways 72 and 93, as well as White Ranch Open Space.  Across the far northeast plains, peak gusts included:  70 mph, 8 miles south of Holyoke; 63 mph, 4 miles west of Peetz; 61 mph near Ovid and 11 miles west-northwest of Westplains; and 59 mph at Holyoke Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured 4 miles west of Peetz.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly flow with enhanced bands of moderate to heavy snow developed across the northern mountains and northeast plains of Colorado. The heaviest snowfall occurred near Rabbit Ears Pass with a storm total of 44 inches, while 40 inches was recorded near Mount Zirkel.  Elsewhere across the northern mountains and in Grand Valley, storm totals ranged from 14 to 32 inches, heaviest in the mountains north of the I-70.  Across the Front Range Foothills storm totals range from 6 to 16 inches.  Over the northeast plains, storm totals from 6 to 12 inches were observed, heaviest along and north of I-76 and near the foothills.  Lesser amounts of 2 to 5 inches were reported to the south of the I-70 and I-76 corridors, and over northeast Weld County and Morgan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  44 inches near Rabbit Rars Pass; 40 inches, 4 miles south of Mount Zirkel; 22 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass; 19 inches, 10 miles northwest of Coalmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped across the region in the wake of a departing system. This led to the development of dense fog across the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Grand Valley, generally centered along the Colorado River, impacting Interstate 70 from the 24 Road exit through Loma. NWS employees in Fruita and the Redlands reported visibility being reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant clearing late in the morning resulted in the development of high instability across western Colorado. This, paired with upper level support and ample moisture, produced strong to severe thunderstorms across the area in the late afternoon and early evening hours. Severe wind gusts up to 61 mph occurred in Garfield County. Additionally, heavy rain occurred with storms which led to flash flooding and debris flows across portions of San Miguel, Montrose, and Mesa counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms on the lower end of the No Thoroughfare Trail between D Road and Monument Road in Grand Junction.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and mudslides across portions of Montrose County, focused along Colorado Highway 90 south of Naturita.

Some stronger winds occurred as the result of decaying outflow boundaries. This led to locally higher winds stronger than 58 mph in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Montrose Regional Airport. These strong winds led to some fence damage in Montrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over west-central Colorado for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 12 inches measured east-southeast of Gypsum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms brought heavy, wind swept snow, to the mountains of West-Central Colorado from mid to late February. This snow fell on an older layer of snow that was deposited in late December 2021, which had become more faceted and weak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of backcountry snowshoers accidentally triggered an avalanche while hiking to a private backcountry home in the Yule Creek area of West-Central Colorado. Three of the hikers and two dogs were caught in the avalanche with one hiker and the two dogs buried. Two others were partially buried. Rescue efforts were unsuccessful with one hiker killed, along with the two dogs. The partially buried hikers survived with minor injuries.

A grassland wildfire in extreme southwest Yuma County Colorado was noticed around 12:15 PM MDT. Multiple fire departments were dispatched and containment of the fire was achieved at 6 PM MDT. Due to strong winds and low relative humidity values, teams were kept on site overnight to combat flare-ups and hot spots. In total, 3,250 acres of grassland and an old, abandoned building were burnt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hotspot was noticed on satellite. Yuma county dispatch was contacted and reported that fire response was seeing smoke and was already enroute to the fire.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SAN JUAN MOUNTAINS ABOVE 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather persisted for most of this period, as several upper level disturbances moved across the region. This setup resulted in widespread heavy snow across all of the higher terrain, as well as lighter accumulations in lower-lying areas. This activity was also accompanied by a massive influx of arctic air, dropping temperatures across the region during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot fell in the past 24-hours, 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave disturbances dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest. This, paired with above normal precipitable water and jet support aloft, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow for Western Colorado. Favorable southwest flow allowed the San Juan Mountains to see the greatest snowfall totals. Snow bands also resulted in locally higher amounts for some areas. While there was a brief break in activity for some areas between systems on the morning of the 22, some areas saw continuous snowfall through the four-day period. Wind gusts exceeded 30 mph in the valleys and gusted to higher than 45 mph in some mountains throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad longwave midlevel trough dug into western Colorado before lifting overhead. The increase in moisture and favorable southwesterly flow resulted in moderate snowfall over the southern mountains and adjacent valleys. Moderate snow also fell in some central mountains and valleys. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times in the mountains with gusts up to 35 mph occurring in some valleys throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the Montrose area, much of that within 12 hours. Winds gusted up to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad longwave midlevel trough dug into western Colorado before lifting overhead. The increase in moisture and favorable southwesterly flow resulted in moderate snowfall over the southern mountains and adjacent valleys. Moderate snow also fell in some central mountains and valleys. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times in the mountains with gusts up to 35 mph occurring in some valleys throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 9 inches reported around Ouray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave disturbances dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest. This, paired with above normal precipitable water and jet support aloft, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow for Western Colorado. Favorable southwest flow allowed the San Juan Mountains to see the greatest snowfall totals. Snow bands also resulted in locally higher amounts for some areas. While there was a brief break in activity for some areas between systems on the morning of the 22, some areas saw continuous snowfall through the four-day period. Wind gusts exceeded 30 mph in the valleys and gusted to higher than 45 mph in some mountains throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of 4 to 5 inches reported near the Colorado state line. Winds gusted to 31 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 25th, an upper air disturbance moved over the High Plains region allowing for snow showers to develop over east-central Colorado during the early morning that lasted into the afternoon hours. During this time, a snow band developed which caused heavy snowfall up to 26 inches in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall due to snow band was reported across Kit Carson county ranging between 8 and 26 inches. The heaviest snowfall of 26 inches was reported via social media in Bethune, CO at 12:30 PM MST. The entire portion of Interstate 70 that runs through the county was closed due to the snowfall. Key impacts: heavy snow, road closures, transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of the afternoon, winds shifted to the northwest across Eastern Colorado. Along with the wind shift, the winds also increased with gusts of 51-66 mph recorded via ASOS/AWOS stations and other mesonet stations in Yuma and Kit Carson counties. With dry soil the winds were able to lift the dirt from fields into the air, producing localized areas of blowing dust with visibility reported as low as 2 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds moved across portions of Eastern Colorado with gusts ranging from 52 to 62 mph. The strongest winds were observed in the city of Burlington. The combination of strong winds and dry fields produced areas of localized blowing dust with visibility reported as low as 3.5 miles during the early part of the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 14th, a cold front passed through east-central Colorado during the late morning and afternoon hours. Winds behind this cold front gusted up to an estimated 80 mph and damaged some property in the region.

A delayed report was received from an observer in Kit Carson, CO that a wind gust bent a 24-foot flag pole about 5 degrees and ripped the flag to pieces. This wind gust was estimated at around 80 mph based on damage.

The heaviest snowfall of 11 inches was received via a delayed CoCoRaHS report at 8 miles north-northeast of Arapahoe, CO. The criteria time based on radar imagery. Key impacts: heavy snow, transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of cold temperatures caused an ice jam to form on the Roaring Fork River in portions of Eagle County. Minor flooding occurred along the river with elevated river flows peaking during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS gage along the Roaring Fork River near Emma, Colorado showed indications of an ice jam release.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave disturbances dropped into the Great Basin from the Pacific Northwest. This, paired with above normal precipitable water and jet support aloft, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow for Western Colorado. Favorable southwest flow allowed the San Juan Mountains to see the greatest snowfall totals. Snow bands also resulted in locally higher amounts for some areas. While there was a brief break in activity for some areas between systems on the morning of the 22, some areas saw continuous snowfall through the four-day period. Wind gusts exceeded 30 mph in the valleys and gusted to higher than 45 mph in some mountains throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of shortwave troughs lifted out of a broad trough anchored over the Pacific Northwest. The shortwave and associated surface cold front tracked over the Northern Rockies and caused the pressure gradient to tighten aloft. Gusty winds accompanied the system with widespread winds of 45 mph reported. There were several gusts of greater than 58 mph in the valleys and greater than 75 mph in the mountains which met high wind criteria across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the Jay RAWS automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure lifted over the Great Basin and caused the pressure gradient to tighten over western Colorado. The presence of a strong jet aloft and deep mixing resulted in widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Several areas directly under the jet saw gusts that exceeded 58 mph in the valley and 75 mph in the mountains, which met high wind criteria. Blowing dust was also a concern in southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough and associated polar jet caused the pressure gradient to tighten over western Colorado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by an automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of February 22nd, an arctic airmass moved into east-central Colorado with breezy northerly winds following a cold front passage in the previous day. Wind chills values as low as -28 degrees Fahrenheit were recorded in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning northerly sustained winds with speeds ranging between 15 to 20 mph combined with morning minimum temperatures as cold as -4 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -28 degrees Fahrenheit in Yuma County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of shortwave troughs lifted out of a broad trough anchored over the Pacific Northwest. The shortwave and associated surface cold front tracked over the Northern Rockies and caused the pressure gradient to tighten aloft. Gusty winds accompanied the system with widespread winds of 45 mph reported. There were several gusts of greater than 58 mph in the valleys and greater than 75 mph in the mountains which met high wind criteria across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 75 MPH was measured by an automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the southwestern United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of western Colorado. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys with gusts of 60 mph or more in the mountains. This led to widespread blowing snow in the higher elevations which, when paired with moderate to heavy snow, resulted in near blizzard conditions. Light to moderate snow also fell in some valley locations as snow levels rapidly dropped with the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 2 feet reported. Winds gusted from 65 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front caused wind gusts to around 45 to 55 mph across southwest Colorado. An area of dust and dirt from northern New Mexico was lifted hundreds of feet into the air, reducing the visibility to less than one mile across portions of Archuleta and La Plata counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large plume of dust was evident across portions of the San Juan River Basin. The Emergency Manager of Archuleta County reported that visibilities of less than one mile occurred in blowing dust as wind gusts exceeded 50 mph. The event was sustained for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the region. This, paired with the presence of a 120 knot jetstreak aloft, resulted in strong winds at the surface. Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph occurred across western Colorado with several areas in west-central and southwest Colorado reporting gusts that exceeded 58 mph, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by an automated weather station at the Durango-La Plata County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed across eastern Elbert, northern Lincoln, Logan, Sedgwick, Phillips and Washington counties.  Peak wind gusts up to 70 mph were observed.  A large tree was knocked down at Columbine Park in Sterling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 68 mph near Julesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds continued across eastern Elbert, Lincoln, Logan, Morgan, Sedgwick, Phillips and Washington counties.  The strongest wind occurred near Sterling with a peak gust to 69 mph.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the southwestern United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of western Colorado. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys with gusts of 60 mph or more in the mountains. This led to widespread blowing snow in the higher elevations which, when paired with moderate to heavy snow, resulted in near blizzard conditions. Light to moderate snow also fell in some valley locations as snow levels rapidly dropped with the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 18 inches estimated at the McCoy Park SNOTEL site. Winds gusted from 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front caused wind gusts to around 45 to 55 mph across southwest Colorado. An area of dust and dirt from northern New Mexico was lifted hundreds of feet into the air, reducing the visibility to less than one mile across portions of Archuleta and La Plata counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large plume of dust was evident across portions of the Animas River Basin. While the sensor at the Durango-La Plata County Airport only reported a minimum visibility of 3 SM, satellite observations showed visibilities were much lower as wind gusts exceeded 50 mph.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems, many of them dry, quickly brushed western Colorado in April which led to precipitation generally trending below normal for the region. This continued below normal precipitation allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged for the majority of the Western Slope. However, areas of extreme drought were confined to the Four Corners Region with lesser categories elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of April. The extreme (D3) drought persisted over the southwest corner with severe (D2) conditions over the central areas and moderate (D1) drought along the northeast corner through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from 58 to 74 mph developed over northeast plains of Colorado during the afternoon. Grassfires developed over Morgan County, one northwest of Wiggins and another just south of Fort Morgan. The fire near Wiggins burned at total of 40 acres.  Colorado Department of Transportation issued a high wind restriction for Interstate 76.  CO34 was closed for a brief time in Morgan County due to the fire.  The smoke severely limited visibilities to near zero and contributed to an accident involving 3 vehicles, all of which were minor.  In Logan County, a downed tree near Sterling damaged two parked vehicles.  Downed power lines near Sterling also sparked a small grassfire.  A fence was blown down and a chicken coop was damaged near Iliff.  Peak wind gusts up to 74 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included  62 mph near Keenseburg and 58 mph at Crow Creek and Hudson.

The smoke severely limited visibilities to near zero and contributed to an accident involving 3 vehicles, all of which were minor. A fence was blown down and a chicken coop was damaged near Iliff.  Peak wind gusts up to 74 mph were observed.

An anomalously strong and unseasonably cold Pacific trough progressed inland across the southwestern United States. Strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of the trough and along the cold front across all of western Colorado. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys with gusts of 60 mph or more in the mountains. Several areas saw gusts meeting high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Dragon Road.

The highest wind gust occurred in Cheyenne County.  The high winds over dry ground caused localized dust storms to occur at several locations.  In addition the largest wildfire so far this year for Kit Carson County occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility was reported six miles north of Wild Horse.

The highest reported wind gust was 61 MPH in Kit Carson County.  The high winds led to dust storm conditions near Aroya, causing Highway 40 to be closed and blew a semi over.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season winter storm moved into Colorado on Thursday, May 20st, bringing much colder temperatures and heavy snow in and near the Colorado Front Range.  Heavy, wet snow accumulated on trees which had already leafed out for the season, resulting in broken tree limbs and scattered power outages. At one point Saturday morning May 22nd, nearly 100,000 customers along the Front Range were without power. Snow accumulated on roads in the higher terrain, and portions of I-70 west of Denver were shut down for a brief period of time. Total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 11 inches across the Boulder and Denver metro areas, to 6 to 18 inches across the Palmer Divide, to between 6 to 24 inches across the foothills and mountains from Boulder County south through Park County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall accumulating 3 to 12 inches over about a day.

Heavy, wet snow accumulated on trees which had already leafed out for the season, resulting in broken tree limbs and scattered power outages. At one point Saturday morning May 22nd, nearly 100,000 customers along the Front Range were without power. Snow accumulated on roads in the higher terrain, and portions of I-70 west of Denver were shut down for a brief period of time. Total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 11 inches across the Boulder and Denver metro areas, to 6 to 18 inches across the Palmer Divide, to between 6 to 24 inches across the foothills and mountains from Boulder County south through Park County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall accumulating 6 to 23 inches over about a day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 75 mph were recorded across Yuma county. The highest wind gust of 75 mph was recorded by a Mesonet station located 4 miles north-northwest of Vernon, CO. Damage from these winds included a public report received from 6 miles south of Idalia, CO that a wind gust tore a 10 foot by 36 foot carport off of their home and lifted it approximately 30 feet over his house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day a strong upper level disturbance centered over the upper Midwest caused wind gusts up to 70 MPH to occur over East Central Colorado.  The highest wind gust occurred in Cheyenne County.  The high winds over dry ground caused localized dust storms to occur at several locations.  In addition the largest wildfire so far this year for Kit Carson County occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the largest fire in the county so far this year.  The fire started at CR 56 and CR R and spread southeast to CR 57 and CR N.  Two outbuildings, 200 trees, a corral with 200 posts and 60 panels, and a tractor were all burned.  The number of acres burned is unknown.  Four different departments worked together to extinguish the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours a low pressure system moved across the Rockies towards the Central Plains, producing high winds with a peak gust of 61 mph and a cluster of thunderstorms that produced mainly wind gusts below 58 mph over eastern Colorado. The peak thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was reported in Yuma, Colorado. The winds combined with the drought conditions in the area also produced blowing dust with visibility reduced as low as one block at times in western Kit Carson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station EW7003 measured two 61 mph gusts out of the northwest over a five minute duration. Storms  were beginning to move across Kit Carson County during the time of observation; however, these gusts were determined to not be storm related.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed little from the previous day of high winds and blowing dust.  The upper level disturbance continued to slide northeast over the Upper Midwest.  High winds rounding the backside of the disturbance were able to reach the ground over East Central Colorado.  The peak gust was 66 MPH at the Burlington airport.  Dust storms also occurred due to the high winds and dry ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 59 MPH were reported across the county.  The highest gust reported was near Firstview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a line of showers and storms developed over Eastern Colorado. As the line move over Yuma and Kit Carson counties, 60 mph wind gusts occurred with some small tree limb damage reported near Idalia, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station KCOCHEYE8 reported a 60 mph wind gust over a duration of five minutes as a storm approached from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a line of showers and storms developed over Eastern Colorado. As the line move over Yuma and Kit Carson counties, 60 mph wind gusts occurred with some small tree limb damage reported near Idalia, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds associated with a thunderstorm snapped one inch diameter limbs off a tree.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall impacted the Front Range mountains and foothills to close out the month.  Above 8 thousand feet,  storm totals generally ranged from 4 to 9 inches, with locally higher amounts.  The big winner in the event was near Blue Valley near Idaho Springs, which picked up 16 inches of snowfall.  Trail Ridge Road was shutdown as snow drifts from 1 to 3 feet covered portions of the scenic highway.   In addition, Colorado Department of Transportation closed Loveland Pass due to safety concerns, and several brief closures occurred along Westbound I-70, from Floyd Hill to Silverthorne.  Along the urban corridor and in the foothills below 8 thousand feet, much needed rainfall up to 1.5 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow forced the closure of Trail Ridge Road, with drifts up to 3 feet.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 8500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of southern Colorado observed higher wind gusts on this day, though the highest gusts remained limited. There were a couple of higher gusts, especially around Westcliffe where a 79 mph gust was observed.

A strong low pressure system developed across the High Plains in the very early morning hours of March 21st. As the low progressed slowly east then northeast, a significant band of heavy snowfall developed across eastern Colorado. This core of the heaviest snow within the band was around 20-30 miles wide, and the impacts within that heaviest snow were extreme. 15 to 18 inches of snow were measured in portions of central Kit Carson County with lower amounts spread out across the rest of eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant snow band produced extreme impacts across central Kit Carson County. Snow totals ranged from 7 inches near Burlington and around 6 inches in the western part of the county to well over a foot in the central part of the county. In fact, several reports of over 12 inches were received, with the highest being a measured 18 inch snow depth in Stratton, CO. Interstate 70 was closed from Limon, Colorado to Goodland, Kansas around 2 pm MDT as a result of the wintry conditions. There were some reports of snow drifts as well. Finally, agriculture impacts were reported in the form of calves being buried in the snow drifts and some perished as a result of the heavy snow. Key impacts: heavy snow, snow drifts, agriculture/cattle losses, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from 58 to 74 mph developed over northeast plains of Colorado during the afternoon. Grassfires developed over Morgan County, one northwest of Wiggins and another just south of Fort Morgan. The fire near Wiggins burned at total of 40 acres.  Colorado Department of Transportation issued a high wind restriction for Interstate 76.  CO34 was closed for a brief time in Morgan County due to the fire.  The smoke severely limited visibilities to near zero and contributed to an accident involving 3 vehicles, all of which were minor.  In Logan County, a downed tree near Sterling damaged two parked vehicles.  Downed power lines near Sterling also sparked a small grassfire.  A fence was blown down and a chicken coop was damaged near Iliff.  Peak wind gusts up to 74 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grassfires developed in Morgan County, one northwest of Wiggins and another just south of Fort Morgan. The wildfire near Wiggins burned at total of 40 acres. Colorado Department of Transportation issued a high wind restriction for Interstate 76. CO34 was closed for a brief time in Morgan County due to the fire. The smoke severely limited visibilities and contributed to an accident involving 3 vehicles, all of which were minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation persisted across much of western Colorado during the month of May. This allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged for the majority of the Western Slope. An exception was across portions of southwest and west-central Colorado which saw the drought worsen by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought worsened across the entirety of the zone through the month of May. The moderate (D1) to severe (D2) drought down south worsened to the extreme (D3) category by the end of the month. Elsewhere, the moderate conditions worsened to the severe category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An rockfall and avalanche occurred near the Dreamweaver Couloir on Mt. Meeker. Three climbers were involved in the incident, a female and two males. One of the male climbers was killed and recovered later that day.  Due to the location and severity of injuries of the other male, Rocky Mountain National Park Search and Rescue requested assistance from a Colorado National Guard helicopter. The female suffered minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three climbers were caught in a rockslide and avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the wake of a shortwave trough allowed for strong radiational cooling across western Colorado. This resulted in subfreezing temperatures which impacted those valleys where vegetation was susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from the upper 20s to low 30s across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Craig-Moffat County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved over Eastern Colorado.  A corridor of high winds from the upper level disturbance reached the ground over Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  The highest reported wind gust was 61 MPH in Kit Carson County.  The high winds led to dust storm conditions near Aroya, causing Highway 40 to be closed and blew a semi over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 61 MPH were reported across the county.  The highest wind gust reported was at the Burlington airport.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility down to five miles for a brief time at the Burlington airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances moving overhead along with a cold front in place supported a prolonged period of snow showers across the higher terrain. A brief period of higher intensity snow did help bring the highest snowfall amounts into the one to two feet range, mainly across the Sangre de Cristo and Wet Mountains. This stronger system also supported higher winds, especially over and near the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow 21.4 inches. Snow core 1.14 inches. 1 west southwest Rosita.

CZ_NAME: TRINIDAD VICINITY / LOWER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN & WESTERN LAS ANIMAS COUNTY BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moving across the region brought high winds to much of southern Colorado. While it was windy everywhere on this day, the strongest winds of 80 to 90 mph occurred over the southern I-25 corridor from just south of Pueblo down to the New Mexico border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trinidad airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed across the High Plains in the very early morning hours of March 21st. As the low progressed slowly east then northeast, a significant band of heavy snowfall developed across eastern Colorado. This core of the heaviest snow within the band was around 20-30 miles wide, and the impacts within that heaviest snow were extreme. 15 to 18 inches of snow were measured in portions of central Kit Carson County with lower amounts spread out across the rest of eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant snowband impacted Yuma County, and more specifically southern and eastern Yuma County. Amounts ranged from around an inch in far northwestern Yuma County to 10 or higher inches across southern Yuma County. The highest amounts reported were 9.5 inches in Wray, Colorado, but with amounts over a foot reported in far northern Kit Carson County, it's quite possible storm totals approached one foot along the southern Yuma County border, between the Joes/Kirk area and Highway 385. Some snow drifts were also observed with this event. Key impacts: heavy snow, snow drifts, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two bands of snow that moved over far East-Central Colorado that created snow accumulations ranging from 3 to 8 inches across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties through the morning hours on the 10th. Cheyenne County received the heaviest snow accumulation of 4 to 8 inches while the other counties received amounts in the 3 to 5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced two bands of moderate snowfall. Snow accumulations across Cheyenne County ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed near the town of Arapahoe, Colorado.   Key impacts:  snow packed roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 19th, a cold front passed through the east-central region of Colorado. Due to this cold front, winds gusted up to around 53 mph causing blowing dust that lowered visibility down to 3 miles within portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KITR ASOS located 5 miles south of Burlington, CO reported a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 53 mph at its location. These winds caused blowing dust that lowered visibility down to 3 miles in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from unknown causes 7 miles north of Pagosa Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Plumtaw Fire began in mid May from unknown causes, 7 miles north of Pagosa Springs, and grew to 721 acres before it was contained in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation persisted across much of western Colorado during the month of May. This allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged for the majority of the Western Slope. An exception was across portions of southwest and west-central Colorado which saw the drought worsen by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought worsened across the entirety of the zone through the month of May. The moderate (D1) to severe (D2) drought degraded to the extreme (D3) category area-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall occurred across the mountains, foothills, Palmer Divide and adjacent plains. Storm total accumulations over one to two days were generally 6 to 18 inches in the mountains, 3 to 12 inches in the foothills, and 3 to 6 inches on the Palmer Divide and adjacent plains east into Lincoln County and north through the Denver metro area into Boulder County. Lesser accumulations of trace to about 3 inches were observed on the northern plains. Other than the Park Range, the greatest accumulations were observed in and around the southern Front Range Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snowfall accumulating 3 to 9 inches over about a day and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall occurred across much of northeast Colorado. Storm total accumulations over about half to a full day were generally 9 to 18 inches in the mountains, 4 to 20 inches in the foothills, 5 to 12 inches on the Palmer Divide, and 1 to 12 inches on the adjacent plains especially north and east into Weld, Morgan and Washington Counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early winter storms dropped heavy snow over the San Juan Mountains in late December 2021 followed by over a month of dry weather into mid February 2022. By mid February, snowfall picked back up across the Colorado High Country with a series of winter storms passing through over the course of several weeks. The dry period in between weakened the snowpack, resulting in hazardous backcountry conditions into the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 2:00 PM on March 17, 2022, a solo backcountry snowboarder was caught, buried, and killed in an avalanche south of Trout Lake, about five miles southwest of the town of Ophir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon a strong cold front moved south through Kit Carson County.  A thunderstorm developed along the front.  Hail up to quarter size was reported with this thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to quarter in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter storms dropped heavy snow over the mountains across Western Colorado in late December 2021 followed by over a month of dry weather into mid February 2022. By mid February, a series of new winter storms brought more heavy snow to the Colorado High Country. The dry period in between weakened the snowpack, resulting in hazardous backcountry conditions into the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 19, two backcountry skiers planned a trip to an area locally known as the Dome in the North Fork of Fish Creek. The area is approximately eight miles east of the town of Steamboat Springs.  While descending into a steeper, more open area, one of the skiers triggered an avalanche, was swept downhill and pinned against a tree.  The second skier witnessed the avalanche and attempted CPR for over one hour before First Responders arrived. All attempts to revive the victim failed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued clearing in the wake of a disturbance resulted in another round of strong radiational cooling across northwest Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures that impacted the lower valleys that had vegetation susceptible to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from the upper 20s to low 30s across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Meeker Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough and associated polar jet caused the pressure gradient to tighten over western Colorado. This resulted in widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. The Debeque to Silt corridor in west-central Colorado valleys saw gusts that exceeded 58 mph, which met high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 MPH was measured by an automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across western Colorado. Gusty outflow winds were the primary threat with activity, particularly the high based showers over northwest Colorado. Some gusts met high wind criteria in the Central Yampa River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to below normal precipitation persisted across much of western Colorado during the month of June. This allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged for the majority of the Western Slope. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed across the High Plains in the very early morning hours of March 21st. As the low progressed slowly east then northeast, a significant band of heavy snowfall developed across eastern Colorado. This core of the heaviest snow within the band was around 20-30 miles wide, and the impacts within that heaviest snow were extreme. 15 to 18 inches of snow were measured in portions of central Kit Carson County with lower amounts spread out across the rest of eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy snowband produced impactful snow across portions of Cheyenne County. The highest measured report received was 6 inches at a location six miles north of Wild Horse. However, based on radar imagery and the slow movement of the heavy snowband, it is likely that higher amounts would have been reported in central Cheyenne County, particularly north central Cheyenne County. Amounts likely exceeded 8 inches in this area, but could have been 12 inches based on reports from central Kit Carson County. There were also some snow drifts observed with this event. Key impacts: heavy snow, snow drifts, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure dominated the Four Corners region. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across much of western Colorado. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to minimum humidities in the single digits to low teens in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 100 to 105 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations. The Grand Junction Regional Airport set a new record high temperature for the day of 102 degrees. A 52-year old man who was mountain biking the end of the Palisade Plunge trail outside of Palisade ran out of water while biking and died as a result of heat-related illness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 17th, a lee surface cyclone moved through eastern Colorado allowing for snow showers to develop in east-central Colorado during the morning and afternoon hours. Snowfall totals from these showers ranged from between 3 and 5.5 inches. Blowing snow was reported due gusty winds around 30 to 40 kts. A few car accidents were reported as a result of these conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the county that estimated between 3 and 5.5 inches based on local reports during the morning and afternoon hours. The highest amount was 5.5 inches of snow reported at 5 NNW Peconic, CO. Local dispatch reported numerous automobile accidents across the county due to hazardous driving conditions caused by the combination of snowfall and gusty winds. No injuries reported, but some motorists taken to local hospital for precautionary purposes. Event time period based on radar data and snowfall reports. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

However, continued below normal precipitation down in southwest Colorado allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of March. The extreme (D3) drought persisted over the southwest corner with severe (D2) conditions over the central areas and moderate (D1) drought along the northeast corner through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several systems moved through western Colorado in March with precipitation generally trending near to above normal for the northern and central areas. This led to some improvement in the drought conditions across northwest Colorado. However, continued below normal precipitation down in southwest Colorado allowed the drought conditions to remain unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across some of the zone through the month of March. The extreme (D3) drought over the northwest corner of the zone improved to the severe (D2) category while severe conditions continued over the central areas. Elsewhere, the moderate (D1) drought persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall, up to 2.5 inches in 20 minutes, produced flash flooding in and around the Denver area.  The heavy rainfall flooded the underpass at 38th and Blake, as well as Interstate 70, from Brighton Blvd to York St.  Denver firefighters rescued 19 people from those locations alone.  Floodwaters knocked down a retaining wall in Thornton at 8600 Washington St.  Flooding and tree damage also occurred at the Denver Zoo. Six to 12 inches of standing water was observed at 47th and Lincoln.  Flooding also observed at 16th and Franklin St.  Intense winds from a wet microburst, downed a tree in Denver which fell onto vehicle parked nearby along 13th St. in Capitol Hill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding as 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rainfall fell in 20 minutes.  Intersections were flooded and vehicles stranded.  The fire department performed several water rescues.  Most of the rescues occurred at the underpass near 38th and Blake St, and along Interstate 70, in a newly renovated section between Brighton Blvd and York St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall, up to  2.6 inches was observed from southeast Denver and Aurora, and extended south southwest to Castle Pines, west of Castle Rock and into Roxborough State Park.  Most of the rainfall fell in less than 45 minutes.   A storm drain at Quincy and Parker was clogged up by rocks that got pulled out from near the sidewalk by running water. Several cars were towed out of the flooded area.  Minor flooding was reported on the Dry Creek exit of I-25 with standing water on Iliff Ave. near Buckley Rd. Parker and Quincy Roads and Dartmouth and Chambers Roads also were impassable.  Multiple accidents occurred due to the rain and flooding.  A few severe storms brought large hail up to quarter size, and strong winds from 60 to 65 mph in Arapahoe, Douglas and Elbert counties.||In Larimer County, flash flooding was reported In the Cameron Peak burn area along  Streamside Drive.  Dangerous flooding is occurring in the area of Glen Haven, including County Road 43 to Drake.  Highway 34 was closed from County Road 29 due to rockslides east of Drake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding.  Several intersections were flooded covered in 6 to 12 inches of water, and resulted in several road closures.

During the evening scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast into East Central Colorado.  Hail up to grapefruit size was reported near Wauneta.  The storms went on to produce wind gusts up to 71 MPH near Idalia.  Wind gusts from the thunderstorms broke off small tree limbs and tossed a pool cover near Bethune.  Behind the severe storms, a large area of moderate rainfall moved in later in the night.  Some of this rainfall fell on ground which had received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days ago.  The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was smaller, with a mixture of half to three quarter inch hailstones.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind and hail, along with torrential rainfall across the I-25 Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Areas hardest hit included:  eastern Boulder, Douglas, eastern Larimer, Washington and Weld counties.  Hail up to tennis ball size (2.5 inches), was observed near Black Hollow Reservoir east of Fort Collins.  Elsewhere the hail ranged from quarter to golfball size.  The hail damaged homes and vehicles.  Strong winds also produced some tree damage.  Torrential rainfall produced extensive street flooding around Fort Collins.  Torrential rain and small hail also occurred in the town of Estes Park. The hail piled up to a foot deep in spots, and had to be removed using snowplows. People in the community helped drivers dig out cars and clear the roadway on East Elkhorn Avenue.  Minor flooding also occurred in some of the nearby businesses.  Minor flash flooding was also observed in Washington County.

Residual monsoon moisture resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across southwest Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows in portions of Gunnison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mudslides occurred along Colorado Highway 149 north of Lake City between mile markers 75-77 as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. The road was closed for several hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across southwest Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. Some storms produced localized debris flows in Montezuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in mud and debris on Road BB near Pleasant View, between roads 12 and 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized flash flooding was reported over the southeast portion of the Cameron Peak burn area near Glen Haven.  Larimer County Road 43 was impassable with floodwaters overtopping the roadway near Streamside Drive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 43 at Streamside Drive was impassable due to floodwaters from Miller Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced six short-lived tornadoes across northeast Weld and Morgan counties.  Two of those landspouts were rated as EF1.  In Weld County, the tornado downed several trees and power poles.  In Morgan County, some oak trees were shredded and a power pole was snapped.  Across Douglas, Elbert and northern Lincoln counties, severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to half dollar size.  Strong thunderstorm wind gusts to 60 mph were reported along highway 71, south of Limon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down and snapped a utility pole near the 24500 block of County Road R.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind and hail, along with torrential rainfall across the I-25 Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Areas hardest hit included:  eastern Boulder, Douglas, eastern Larimer, Washington and Weld counties.  Hail up to tennis ball size (2.5 inches), was observed near Black Hollow Reservoir east of Fort Collins.  Elsewhere the hail ranged from quarter to golfball size.  The hail damaged homes and vehicles.  Strong winds also produced some tree damage.  Torrential rainfall produced extensive street flooding around Fort Collins.  Torrential rain and small hail also occurred in the town of Estes Park. The hail piled up to a foot deep in spots, and had to be removed using snowplows. People in the community helped drivers dig out cars and clear the roadway on East Elkhorn Avenue.  Minor flooding also occurred in some of the nearby businesses.  Minor flash flooding was also observed in Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 15th, thunderstorms developed in east-central Colorado due to a surface trough in the area. One of these thunderstorms became severe and produced wind gusts up to an estimated 60 mph and pea sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of an estimated 50 to 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust, heavy rain that the reporter's windshield wipers could not keep up with, and pea sized hail at location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of July 26, 2022, strong to severe storms developed along a stationary frontal boundary across eastern Colorado. Storms initially fired across southern Yuma County, but then proceeded to build southwest along the boundary. A favorable environment for tornado development was in place and several tornadoes were able to occur in Kit Carson County, Colorado. In addition, hail up to half dollar size was received with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout tornado southeast of Kirk, Colorado in far northern Kit Carson County. Tornado duration was around 3 minutes. No damage. Locations and times estimated from both spotter/first responder reports and NWS Goodland radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms that had developed in northern Colorado moved east into northeastern Colorado. As these storms moved closer to the Colorado and Kansas/Nebraska borders, they began decaying. As these storms decayed, a heat burst occurred which caused severe winds which led to a hay shed being tossed around 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of an un-anchored hay shed flipped and thrown about 100 yards due to wind. Estimated size of the barn is 12X20 feet. Speed is estimated based on nearby measured gusts and the building that was thrown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of rainfall slowly progressed east across East Central CO beginning in the late afternoon.  The first wave of rainfall included hail up to dime size that was reported north-northwest of Flagler.  Rain rates of over two inches per hour were reported near Flagler.  Total rainfall was up to four inches.  The prolonged torrential rainfall caused flooding in low lying areas over the west half of Kit Carson County, which lasted into the next day.  The rainfall was enough to fill the Flagler dam to capacity in one day. Meanwhile, as the event progressed a large area of rotation developed over Cheyenne County CO, moving northeast.  This allowed for an enhanced corridor of severe wind gusts to develop.  These winds uprooted a tree in Kit Carson, CO.  The strongest gust reported was 67 MPH near Firstview.

A grouping of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and moved eastward. The leading edge of these storms produced severe winds and hail. These winds caused dust to blow, which reduced visibility to 1/4 mile, and wind driven hail damage to a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media of blowing dust reducing visibility to about or less than 1/4 mile. The blowing dust lasted about 10 minutes. Wind speeds were estimated based on the closest personal weather station near Firstview, CO and the severity of the blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of July 26, 2022, strong to severe storms developed along a stationary frontal boundary across eastern Colorado. Storms initially fired across southern Yuma County, but then proceeded to build southwest along the boundary. A favorable environment for tornado development was in place and several tornadoes were able to occur in Kit Carson County, Colorado. In addition, hail up to half dollar size was received with this event.

During the evening scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast into East Central Colorado.  Hail up to grapefruit size was reported near Wauneta.  The storms went on to produce wind gusts up to 71 MPH near Idalia.  Wind gusts from the thunderstorms broke off small tree limbs and tossed a pool cover near Bethune.  Behind the severe storms, a large area of moderate rainfall moved in later in the night.  Some of this rainfall fell on ground which had received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days ago.  The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a picture of a hailstone next to a tape measure.  At a maximum diameter the hail stone was roughly the size of a grapefruit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy rainfall caused mud and rocks to cover Highway 125, as anywhere from 0.25 to 0.50 inches of rain fell in 30 minutes.  The highway was closed between Trail Creek and Coral Cabin Creek Road (FR 112).  There was some road damage with a large area of mud and rocks covering the roadway.  Two bulldozers were brought out to remove the debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 125 was closed when a brief burst of rain caused a mudslide between Trail Creek and Forest Road 112.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced six short-lived tornadoes across northeast Weld and Morgan counties.  Two of those landspouts were rated as EF1.  In Weld County, the tornado downed several trees and power poles.  In Morgan County, some oak trees were shredded and a power pole was snapped.  Across Douglas, Elbert and northern Lincoln counties, severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to half dollar size.  Strong thunderstorm wind gusts to 60 mph were reported along highway 71, south of Limon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout touched down briefly in an open field.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on the 6th, isolated storms developed over Eastern Colorado and evolved into a line of storms that moved east into Northwest Kansas through the night. These storms produced wind gusts estimated up to 60 mph across Cheyenne County, Colorado along with up to penny sized hail and heavy rain up to 4.5 inches. The heaviest rainfall amounts were reported in the eastern half of Cheyenne County with reports of visibility as low as one-eight of a mile at times. The heavy rain also caused some road flooding on a county road southwest of Arapahoe, Colorado that lasted through the late morning hours on the 8th. No damage was reported from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail sizes ranged from half inch to penny in size.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system and associated surface cold front progressed into the Great Basin and lifted across western Colorado. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the mountains. Locally moderate snow fell in some valley areas as a result of banding along the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced strong thunderstorm winds across portions of Arapahoe, Boulder and Larimer County.  The wind gusts ranged from 60 to 70 mph, with hail up to quarter size reported.  A wind knocked down some large tree limbs in Arapahoe County, and the hail caused minor property damage.

Severe thunderstorms produced strong thunderstorm winds across portions of Arapahoe, Boulder and Larimer County.  The wind gusts ranged from 60 to 70 mph, with hail up to quarter size reported.  A wind knocked down some large tree limbs in Arapahoe County, and the hail caused minor property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm also produced nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people were killed after heavy rain which produced a destructive debris flow in the Cameron Peak burn area.  The 37-yr old woman and her 12-yr old daughter were in a camp trailer that was swept away at Calcite Road along Sheep Creek.  In addition, a cabin along Granite Road was destroyed in the flood. Buckhorn Rd (LCR 44H) was heavily damaged in the flood. Several other roads were washed out or extensively damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were killed when very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Cameron Peak burn area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the Glen Haven and Crystal Mountain areas heavy rain produced flash flooding.  Radar estimated 0.50 to 0.75 inch of rain in 30 minutes.  Miller Fork washed over Larimer County Road 43 at Streamside Drive near The Retreat, closing the roadway.  Streamside Drive was damaged but passable.  Black Creek Drive in The Retreat was damaged pretty significantly.  Every stream crossing along Black Creek Drive was reportedly washed out.  There was a 6-foot drop at the first stream crossing on that road. Buckhorn Creek washed over Wildsong Road, and Sheep Creek washed across LCR 44H with minor damage reported.   Deer Path and Wildsong became impassable by vehicle.||In Morgan County, heavy rain from 1.5 to 3.5 inches produced flash flooding.  A couple of county roads around Brush were flooded.  One of those roads was completely washed out.  The basement of a residence was also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding damaged roads around The Retreat, Glen Haven and Crystal Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample monsoon moisture paired with the aided support aloft from a passing shortwave resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to a localized debris flows and flash flooding in portions of San Miguel and Mesa counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing in normally dry ravines near the east end of the Colorado National Monument as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind and hail, along with torrential rainfall across the I-25 Corridor and Northeast Plains.  Areas hardest hit included:  eastern Boulder, Douglas, eastern Larimer, Washington and Weld counties.  Hail up to tennis ball size (2.5 inches), was observed near Black Hollow Reservoir east of Fort Collins.  Elsewhere the hail ranged from quarter to golfball size.  The hail damaged homes and vehicles.  Strong winds also produced some tree damage.  Torrential rainfall produced extensive street flooding around Fort Collins.  Torrential rain and small hail also occurred in the town of Estes Park. The hail piled up to a foot deep in spots, and had to be removed using snowplows. People in the community helped drivers dig out cars and clear the roadway on East Elkhorn Avenue.  Minor flooding also occurred in some of the nearby businesses.  Minor flash flooding was also observed in Washington County.

A large area of slow moving light to moderate rainfall moved through East Central Colorado during the morning into the early afternoon.  A couple rounds of prolonged moderate rainfall moved over eastern Yuma County during this timeframe.  Close to three inches of rainfall occurred.  The rainfall caused water to flow over Highway 385 almost a half mile north of the Highway 385/36 intersection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 12 inches of water was reported over the roadway approximately a half mile north of the Hwy 385/36 intersection.  The culvert underneath the highway was not able to handle all the runoff from the rainfall.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flash flood was observed along Cedar Creek to the confluence of the Big Thompson River and Highway 34. Debris accumulated behind a bridge along an access road to a neighborhood on Cedar Brook Lane. A significant debris flow continued down Cedar Creek to Cedar Lane.  Fifteen residents were stranded temporarily. Minor flooding was observed along a private road near Masonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood was observed along Cedar Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across southwest and west-central Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result of the heavy rain, a debris flow occurred in Ouray County along Red Mountain Pass and on the west side of Parachute in Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow of mud and small to medium-sized rocks occurred on Highway 6 on the west side of Parachute as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. The road was closed for a couple of hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of rainfall slowly progressed east across East Central CO beginning in the late afternoon.  The first wave of rainfall included hail up to dime size that was reported north-northwest of Flagler.  Rain rates of over two inches per hour were reported near Flagler.  Total rainfall was up to four inches.  The prolonged torrential rainfall caused flooding in low lying areas over the west half of Kit Carson County, which lasted into the next day.  The rainfall was enough to fill the Flagler dam to capacity in one day. Meanwhile, as the event progressed a large area of rotation developed over Cheyenne County CO, moving northeast.  This allowed for an enhanced corridor of severe wind gusts to develop.  These winds uprooted a tree in Kit Carson, CO.  The strongest gust reported was 67 MPH near Firstview.

On the afternoon of July 26, 2022, strong to severe storms developed along a stationary frontal boundary across eastern Colorado. Storms initially fired across southern Yuma County, but then proceeded to build southwest along the boundary. A favorable environment for tornado development was in place and several tornadoes were able to occur in Kit Carson County, Colorado. In addition, hail up to half dollar size was received with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Garage building was shifted east 6 inches off the foundation. A communications tower just east of the garage was folded in half facing the east-northeast. Metal roof panels and pieces of siding were scattered east and west from the garage. A propane tank on the west side of the property was rolled to the south off its base. A playground set on the southwest corner of the house was ripped apart and scattered to the north. A trampoline was crumbled and blown to the northeast. Pieces of siding from the west facing side of the house were scattered across the property. The tornado then traveled west-southwest across Interstate 70 before dissipating.

A grouping of thunderstorms formed in east central Colorado and moved eastward. The leading edge of these storms produced severe winds and hail. These winds caused dust to blow, which reduced visibility to 1/4 mile, and wind driven hail damage to a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via social media of wind driven hail damage to vinyl siding on a house. Estimated the hail size based on the picture of holes in the siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced six short-lived tornadoes across northeast Weld and Morgan counties.  Two of those landspouts were rated as EF1.  In Weld County, the tornado downed several trees and power poles.  In Morgan County, some oak trees were shredded and a power pole was snapped.  Across Douglas, Elbert and northern Lincoln counties, severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to half dollar size.  Strong thunderstorm wind gusts to 60 mph were reported along highway 71, south of Limon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Hill Cemetery outside of Ridgway experienced flooding as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms.
