EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across southwest Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result of the heavy rain, a debris flow occurred in Ouray County on Imogene Pass with some other mudslides reported in Archuleta County. Additionally, gusty outflow winds in excess of 60 mph occurred across portions of Rio Blanco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mud and rockslide occurred on the east side of Imogene Pass as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. The road was closed for a period for cleanup operations.

During the evening scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast into East Central Colorado.  Hail up to grapefruit size was reported near Wauneta.  The storms went on to produce wind gusts up to 71 MPH near Idalia.  Wind gusts from the thunderstorms broke off small tree limbs and tossed a pool cover near Bethune.  Behind the severe storms, a large area of moderate rainfall moved in later in the night.  Some of this rainfall fell on ground which had received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days ago.  The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smaller tree limbs of an unspecified size were blown down and a pool cover of unknown size was tossed.  These winds also occurred with golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on the 6th, isolated storms developed over Eastern Colorado and evolved into a line of storms that moved east into Northwest Kansas through the night. These storms produced wind gusts estimated up to 60 mph across Cheyenne County, Colorado along with up to penny sized hail and heavy rain up to 4.5 inches. The heaviest rainfall amounts were reported in the eastern half of Cheyenne County with reports of visibility as low as one-eight of a mile at times. The heavy rain also caused some road flooding on a county road southwest of Arapahoe, Colorado that lasted through the late morning hours on the 8th. No damage was reported from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of storms moving across Cheyenne County produced wind gusts up to 60 mph east of Kit Carson, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms that had developed in northern Colorado moved east into northeastern Colorado. As these storms moved closer to the Colorado and Kansas/Nebraska borders, they began decaying. As these storms decayed, a heat burst occurred which caused severe winds which led to a hay shed being tossed around 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station in Wray, CO reported sustained winds of 41 mph with gusts up to 59 mph.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent monsoon moisture during the month of July resulted in above normal precipitation for much of western Colorado. This allowed the drought conditions to improve across the San Juan Mountains in particular. Elsewhere, conditions remained unchanged for the majority of the Western Slope through the end of the month. The only area of D3 (extreme) drought was the western half of the Four Corners / Upper Dolores River Basin zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of July. The extreme (D3) drought over the western half of the zone continued while severe (D2) conditions persisted across the eastern areas through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values in excess of 150% of normal occurred across western Colorado as monsoon moisture continued to stream into the region. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed. Weak steering flow aloft resulted in slow moving storms. Additionally, storms produced heavy rain. Flash flooding and a debris flow occurred in Ouray County as a result of the heavy rain. Another localized debris flow occurred in Rio Blanco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred along County Road 5 near mile marker 12 near Piceance Creek as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across southwest and west-central Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. As a result of the heavy rain, a debris flow occurred in Ouray County along Red Mountain Pass and on the west side of Parachute in Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred along Colorado Highway 550 on Red Mountain Pass near Ironton as a result of heavy rain. The Department of Transportation reported approximately 3 feet of mud and debris including dead trees, limbs and boulders on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values in excess of 150% of normal occurred across western Colorado as monsoon moisture continued to stream into the region. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed. Weak steering flow aloft resulted in slow moving storms. Additionally, storms produced heavy rain. Flash flooding and a debris flow occurred in Ouray County as a result of the heavy rain. Another localized debris flow occurred in Rio Blanco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and a debris flow across County Road 361, specifically in the Senator Gulch/Yankee Boy Basin area. Ouray County Road and Bridge reported a critical Hilfiker wall and culvert were significantly damaged as a result of the mudslide. Approximately 50 people were unable to leave the Yankee Boy Basin area until the debris was cleared.

The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pine tree approximately 10 inches in diameter was snapped off by the winds, and a cedar tree was broken into pieces.  There was also an unspecified amount of damage done to some buildings.  The winds occurred with hail up to limes in size.  A total of 1.75 inches of rain occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall occurred for all three counties in East Central Colorado during July.    Rainfall amounts ranged from three to ten inches, with the highest amounts occurring in western Kit Carson County.  The above normal rainfall caused Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties to improvement the drought status.  Despite the above normal rainfall Yuma County drought status worsened to D3 Exceptional Drought for part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from three to eight inches.  This was one to eight inches above normal.  The normal precipitation amount is two to four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained over the Four Corners region. This led to another round of showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. Some storms produced localized debris flows in Pitkin and Ouray counties with flooding reported in Montrose County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud and debris flowed across County Road 17 at Corbett Creek near the Dallas Trailhead as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms. The Corbett Creek culvert was completely plugged. The road was closed for several hours due to cleanup operations.

Storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain. One such storm wiped out a portion of the road leading from the El Diente Peak trailhead from flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While performing a search and rescue operation for an injured hiker at El Diente Peak, Dolores County Search and Rescue members narrowly escaped a portion of the road exiting the trailhead being washed out as a result of flash flooding from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail.  In Larimer County, the wind knocked down three trees and snapped branches.  In Washington County, hail up to quarter size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail.  In Larimer County, the wind knocked down three trees and snapped branches.  In Washington County, hail up to quarter size was reported.

In the Glen Haven and Crystal Mountain areas heavy rain produced flash flooding.  Radar estimated 0.50 to 0.75 inch of rain in 30 minutes.  Miller Fork washed over Larimer County Road 43 at Streamside Drive near The Retreat, closing the roadway.  Streamside Drive was damaged but passable.  Black Creek Drive in The Retreat was damaged pretty significantly.  Every stream crossing along Black Creek Drive was reportedly washed out.  There was a 6-foot drop at the first stream crossing on that road. Buckhorn Creek washed over Wildsong Road, and Sheep Creek washed across LCR 44H with minor damage reported.   Deer Path and Wildsong became impassable by vehicle.||In Morgan County, heavy rain from 1.5 to 3.5 inches produced flash flooding.  A couple of county roads around Brush were flooded.  One of those roads was completely washed out.  The basement of a residence was also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from 1.9 to 3.50 inches was observed near Brush.  Flooding was reported along County Road U, just west of Highway 71.  A basement in the area also flooded.  County Road 28, north of Brush, was completely washed out and underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on the 6th, isolated storms developed over Eastern Colorado and evolved into a line of storms that moved east into Northwest Kansas through the night. These storms produced wind gusts estimated up to 60 mph across Cheyenne County, Colorado along with up to penny sized hail and heavy rain up to 4.5 inches. The heaviest rainfall amounts were reported in the eastern half of Cheyenne County with reports of visibility as low as one-eight of a mile at times. The heavy rain also caused some road flooding on a county road southwest of Arapahoe, Colorado that lasted through the late morning hours on the 8th. No damage was reported from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of storms slowly moved over eastern Cheyenne County producing rainfall amounts up to 4.5 inches of rain. The heaviest rain was reported roughly 7 miles northwest of Firstview, Colorado with amounts up to 3.25 inches reported in area of Arapahoe and Cheyenne Wells, Colorado. Roughly 5 miles southwest of Arapahoe, heavy rain accumulated in a creek that eventually caused flooding over portions of County Road 51. The water depth was estimated to be up to 6 inches. No damage was reported from the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight southwest gradient persisted aloft as a broad trough remained situated over the western states. This led to widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph across western Colorado. Some stronger gusts met high wind criteria, or exceeded 58 mph, in the Lower Yampa River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Dragon Road RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow moving Pacific trough and associated surface cold front finally progressed into the Great Basin late in the night and into the early morning hours. This led to widespread gusts that exceeded 40 mph across the area with numerous stations hitting high wind criteria, or more than 58 mph in the valleys. Virga showers only enhanced these strong surface winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Garfield County Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously cold upper level trough dropped south from the Northern Rockies into western Colorado. A strong cold front associated with the trough pushed across the area resulting in gusty winds to almost 60 mph in the mountains and greater than 40 mph in the valleys. Additionally, the front brought moderate to heavy snow to most mountain ranges in addition to some lower valleys across the northern and central areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area, much of that in 12 hours, with up to 20 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 48 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast into East Central Colorado.  Hail up to grapefruit size was reported near Wauneta.  The storms went on to produce wind gusts up to 71 MPH near Idalia.  Wind gusts from the thunderstorms broke off small tree limbs and tossed a pool cover near Bethune.  Behind the severe storms, a large area of moderate rainfall moved in later in the night.  Some of this rainfall fell on ground which had received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days ago.  The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall was 1.23 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three to five inches of rainfall occurred in the area of Bethune and Stratton.  This area also received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days earlier.  Dispatch received three different reports of one to two feet of water over county roads in this area.  In two of these reports the water extended for 10 to 20 feet over the road.  No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic outbreak brought some of the coldest temperatures to northeast Colorado in nearly 30 years.  The arctic outbreak impacted the Rockies, the Midwest, Northeast, and Southern United States. Denver dropped to -24F, one-degree shy of the all-time December record of -25F set back in 1990.  There was also a 75-degree drop, one-degree shy of the all-time 2-day record temperature differential.  Across north-central and northeast Colorado, wind chill temperatures ranged from -25F to -54F.  In the city of Denver, there was one confirmed fatality.||Denver opened up several more warming shelters. Power outages impacted up to 6400 customers in the I-25 Corridor.  Numerous school districts and county government buildings were closed on the 22nd.  At Denver International Airport,  645 flights were canceled and another 922 were delayed on the 22nd.  The following day, 264 flights were canceled and 253 flights were delayed.  Denver firefighters responded to hundreds of calls related to burst pipes in buildings and homes following the arctic freeze.  Ten buildings on the University of Colorado Boulder campus were impacted by a power interruption, which resulted in widespread damage that included flooding from burst pipes.  At the Dave Skaggs Research Center, extensive water damage occurred from burst pipes.||Portions of I-25, CO 14, US 287, and US 85 were closed due to squalls and blowing snow near the Wyoming border.  Other closures included:  CO 14 from Highway 85 to Sterling, US 6 Sterling to Nebraska state line, CO 113 Highway 138 to Nebraska state line, CO 59 I-76 to Yuma, CO 61 I-76 to Otis, CO 385 Julesburg to Holyoke.  Storm totals in the mountains and foothills ranged from 4 to 13 inches, highest at Eldora Ski Area.  Across the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  At Denver International Airport, 3.9 inches of snowfall was observed, with 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service Office in Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from -25F to -45F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening low pressure system coming off the Rockies caused surface pressures to drop around 14 mb in 24 hours. This drop in air pressure created a strong pressure gradient that caused strong winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site near Firstview, CO measured a gust of 62 mph.

The arctic outbreak impacted the Rockies, the Midwest, Northeast, and Southern United States. Denver dropped to -24F, one-degree shy of the all-time December record of -25F set back in 1990.  There was also a 75-degree drop, one-degree shy of the all-time 2-day record temperature differential.  Across north-central and northeast Colorado, wind chill temperatures ranged from -25F to -54F.  In the city of Denver, there was one confirmed fatality.||Denver opened up several more warming shelters. Power outages impacted up to 6400 customers in the I-25 Corridor.  Numerous school districts and county government buildings were closed on the 22nd.  At Denver International Airport,  645 flights were canceled and another 922 were delayed on the 22nd.  The following day, 264 flights were canceled and 253 flights were delayed.  Denver firefighters responded to hundreds of calls related to burst pipes in buildings and homes following the arctic freeze.  Ten buildings on the University of Colorado Boulder campus were impacted by a power interruption, which resulted in widespread damage that included flooding from burst pipes.  At the Dave Skaggs Research Center, extensive water damage occurred from burst pipes.||Portions of I-25, CO 14, US 287, and US 85 were closed due to squalls and blowing snow near the Wyoming border.  Other closures included:  CO 14 from Highway 85 to Sterling, US 6 Sterling to Nebraska state line, CO 113 Highway 138 to Nebraska state line, CO 59 I-76 to Yuma, CO 61 I-76 to Otis, CO 385 Julesburg to Holyoke.  Storm totals in the mountains and foothills ranged from 4 to 13 inches, highest at Eldora Ski Area.  Across the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  At Denver International Airport, 3.9 inches of snowfall was observed, with 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service Office in Boulder.

A closed low pressure system and associated surface cold front progressed into the Great Basin and lifted across western Colorado. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow across the mountains. Locally moderate snow fell in some valley areas as a result of banding along the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts close to two feet estimated at the Bear Town SNOTEL site.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds occurred out ahead of and along an unseasonably strong cold front that passed across western Colorado. Widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred. Some stronger winds occurred with locally higher winds stronger than 58 mph in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin as well as the Four Corners/Upper Dolores River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Cortez Municipal Airport.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds occurred out ahead of and along an unseasonably strong cold front that passed across western Colorado. Widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred. Some stronger winds occurred with locally higher winds stronger than 58 mph in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin as well as the Four Corners/Upper Dolores River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Montrose Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with a weak Atmospheric River paired with a passing cold front resulted in moderate snow for the mountains of western Colorado and some higher northern valleys. Strong winds occurred ahead of and along the front with gusts up to 50 mph reported at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 16 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of tropical system Kay began to infiltrate the Four Corners region. This resulted in the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across southwest Colorado. Hail three quarters of an inch in diameter fell with a storm in La Plata County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail fell 4 miles south of Vallecito in La Plata County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front progressed across western Colorado. This led to minor street flooding and flash flooding across portions of Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on the lower portion of the Palisade Plunge trail in Mesa County as a result of heavy rain from thunderstorms.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive crash occurred as freezing temperatures and icy conditions followed the first snowfall of the season, most of which fell the previous evening.  Hazardous conditions developed in Denver along an elevated portion of 6th Avenue during the early morning commute.  The crash involved approximately 100 vehicles.  There were no serious injuries, although one driver had to be extricated from a vehicle. The accidents occurred between Kalamath Street and  Federal Boulevard.   Numerous tow trucks took damaged or inoperable vehicles to a parking lot outside Empower Field at Mile High. The icy road conditions made it impossible for drivers to prevent from slamming into each other.  A major cleanup had to take place before 6th was reopened later in the morning.  The closure lasted approximately 5 hours.  Several crashes occurred along Interstate 25 and 70, and included a multi-vehicle accident on the onramp from US 36 to I-25.   Storm totals in the mountains and foothills generally ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  At the National Weather Service in Boulder, 6.6 inches of snowfall was observed.  At Denver International Airport, 4.5 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions resulted in a 100-car pileup along 6th Ave.  It forced the closure of the roadway for several hours.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive crash occurred as freezing temperatures and icy conditions followed the first snowfall of the season, most of which fell the previous evening.  Hazardous conditions developed in Denver along an elevated portion of 6th Avenue during the early morning commute.  The crash involved approximately 100 vehicles.  There were no serious injuries, although one driver had to be extricated from a vehicle. The accidents occurred between Kalamath Street and  Federal Boulevard.   Numerous tow trucks took damaged or inoperable vehicles to a parking lot outside Empower Field at Mile High. The icy road conditions made it impossible for drivers to prevent from slamming into each other.  A major cleanup had to take place before 6th was reopened later in the morning.  The closure lasted approximately 5 hours.  Several crashes occurred along Interstate 25 and 70, and included a multi-vehicle accident on the onramp from US 36 to I-25.   Storm totals in the mountains and foothills generally ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  At the National Weather Service in Boulder, 6.6 inches of snowfall was observed.  At Denver International Airport, 4.5 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals varied anywhere from from 1.5 to 7 inches, with 6.6 inches observed at the National Weather Service in Boulder.

Eight vehicles and fifteen occupants were stranded as a result of the debris flows. The pass and roads remained closed for several days for cleanup operations.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche caught 4 backcountry skiers, two were buried, one was killed. A large avalanche near the summit of Berthoud Pass in an area locally known as Nitro Chute. Nitro Chute is an east-facing avalanche path around 11,500 feet. Two of the riders were able to stay on the surface; the other two riders were completely buried. The party was able to locate both buried riders using their avalanche transceivers. One of the riders was breathing when he was uncovered. The other was not. The party, along with a group of bystanders, performed CPR, but their efforts were unsuccessful. Deputies from the Grand County Sheriff's department, along with Grand County SAR, were able to recover the victim before sundown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche completely buried a backcountry skier near Berthoud Pass. He died at the scene of the incident.

A father and his adult son were caught in the avalanche.  The father was partially buried and the son fully buried.  The father was able to dig himself out and had to ski out of the area to get cell phone reception for a 911 call.  The son was found later in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man died in an avalanche outside the Breckenridge Ski Resort boundary on Peak 10.

Light to moderate snow also fell in some of the valley areas. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 36 inches of snow fell at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure progressed across the Northern Rockies and lifted across western Colorado. Ample moisture and favorable orographics led to moderate to heavy snow across the mountains over a prolonged period. Moderate accumulations also occurred in some valley locations. Winds gusted in excess of 45 mph in the mountains and more than 30 mph in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the area.

An impressive arctic air mass accompanied a strong cold front that dropped across western Colorado. The lift from the front itself paired with strong jet support aloft and minor moisture resulted in scattered showers across the Continental Divide. While snowfall totals weren't overly impressive with this system, generally light to moderate, the combination of snowfall with wind gusts in excess of 70 mph at times in the higher terrain resulted in blizzard-like conditions and significant travel impacts.||In the wake of the system, the arctic air overhead paired with continued breezy conditions resulted in bitterly cold wind chills for the northern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to over 45 mph at times which resulted in significantly reduced visibility in blowing snow and hazardous travel. The cold front brought wind chills down to -37 degrees F near Elk Springs.

While snowfall totals weren't overly impressive with this system, generally light to moderate, the combination of snowfall with wind gusts in excess of 70 mph at times in the higher terrain resulted in blizzard-like conditions and significant travel impacts.||In the wake of the system, the arctic air overhead paired with continued breezy conditions resulted in bitterly cold wind chills for the northern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 90 mph at times which resulted in significantly reduced visibility in blowing snow and hazardous travel. There was a peak wind gust of over 100 mph recorded at the automated station at Carbonate Hill east-northeast of Crested Butte. Generally 1 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 7 inches estimated. The cold front brought wind chills down to -34 degrees F at Monarch Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon of June 24th, a single cell thunderstorm formed in eastern Colorado, near the Colorado and Kansas border. This storm split into two with the right section heading slightly north of east into Greely County, KS while the left section moved north-northwest into Cheyenne County, CO.  The Cheyenne County storms produced hail up to nickel size. As these storms began dissipating, a line of thunderstorms formed in northeast Colorado and moved east-northeast. These storms extended from east central Colorado into west central Nebraska. Once the line of developing storms caught up to the remnants of the first storms, the line intensified. Over the next few hours in Colorado these storms produced more nickel sized hail and winds up to 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter called in a report of dime to nickel sized hail. The hail lasted for about 3 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man drowned in Dillon Reservoir after a microburst struck early in the afternoon.  The strong winds produced large waves that separated a man from his paddle board. When the microburst hit, they saw the man get knocked off of his board.  He was not wearing a personal flotation device and was unable to keep his head above the choppy water.  Members of his group saw the incoming storm and tried to return to the safety of the Marina, but it arrived quicker than they could exit the lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man drowned when strong microburst winds knocked him off his paddle board.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Spring-like snow storm produced a heavy wet snowfall across the Front Range Foothills, I-25 Corridor and adjacent plains.  The storm started with a period of light to moderate rainfall late in the afternoon, then transitioned to very heavy snow during the evening hours.  Snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour were observed.  Much of the snow fell in a occurred in the 6-8 hour time frame, with storm totals ranging from 5 to 14 inches.  The official snowfall measurement at Denver International Airport was 7.1 inches, with 9.8 inches measured at the National Weather Service Office in Boulder.  The heavy wet snow produced scattered outages across the urban corridor.  Numerous branches snapped and damaged parked vehicles.  Multiple car accidents as well, with many vehicles getting stuck in ditches during the peak period of the heaviest snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included: 8.5 inches near Aurora, Buckley AFB, Cherry Hills Village, and Westminster; 7.5 inches near Edgewater, 7.1 inches at Denver International Airport, and 6 inches near Englewood and Federal Heights.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern persisted during much of the month of September. This resulted in above normal precipitation for much of western Colorado. This allowed the drought conditions to improve in some areas, particularly down in southwest Colorado. Elsewhere, conditions remained relatively unchanged through the end of the month. There were no areas of D3 (extreme) drought in the region by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved during the month of September. The extreme (D3) drought over the far southwest corner of the zone improved to the severe (D2) category. Elsewhere, the severe conditions improved to the moderate (D1) category across all but the far southwest corner by the end of the month.

The presence of monsoon moisture paired with the passing of a trough aloft led to widespread showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Several storms produced heavy rain. Additionally, severe thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds in across some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measured to be the size of quarters accumulated on the road in Mesa Verde National Park. The accumulation was estimated to be 6 inches deep. The hail dimpled at least two vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture advected north into the region. Residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across western Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flash flooding across portions of La Plata County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five homes were flooded with water, mud and debris after 2 to 3 inches of rain overwhelmed the culvert system. Over a foot of water was estimated to be in a basement in a home on Lupine Drive. Homes near the intersection of Mesa Avenue and Mustang Street had 4 feet of water close to cresting in their area. The neighboring portion of Colorado Highway 160 had some road debris as a result of the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast into East Central Colorado.  Hail up to grapefruit size was reported near Wauneta.  The storms went on to produce wind gusts up to 71 MPH near Idalia.  Wind gusts from the thunderstorms broke off small tree limbs and tossed a pool cover near Bethune.  Behind the severe storms, a large area of moderate rainfall moved in later in the night.  Some of this rainfall fell on ground which had received a couple inches of rainfall a couple days ago.  The additional three to five inches of rainfall caused flooding to occur in the central part of Kit Carson County which lasted into the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to nickel in size.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive crash occurred as freezing temperatures and icy conditions followed the first snowfall of the season, most of which fell the previous evening.  Hazardous conditions developed in Denver along an elevated portion of 6th Avenue during the early morning commute.  The crash involved approximately 100 vehicles.  There were no serious injuries, although one driver had to be extricated from a vehicle. The accidents occurred between Kalamath Street and  Federal Boulevard.   Numerous tow trucks took damaged or inoperable vehicles to a parking lot outside Empower Field at Mile High. The icy road conditions made it impossible for drivers to prevent from slamming into each other.  A major cleanup had to take place before 6th was reopened later in the morning.  The closure lasted approximately 5 hours.  Several crashes occurred along Interstate 25 and 70, and included a multi-vehicle accident on the onramp from US 36 to I-25.   Storm totals in the mountains and foothills generally ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  At the National Weather Service in Boulder, 6.6 inches of snowfall was observed.  At Denver International Airport, 4.5 inches was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 4 to 7.5 inches.

CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Spring-like snow storm produced a heavy wet snowfall across the Front Range Foothills, I-25 Corridor and adjacent plains.  The storm started with a period of light to moderate rainfall late in the afternoon, then transitioned to very heavy snow during the evening hours.  Snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour were observed.  Much of the snow fell in a occurred in the 6-8 hour time frame, with storm totals ranging from 5 to 14 inches.  The official snowfall measurement at Denver International Airport was 7.1 inches, with 9.8 inches measured at the National Weather Service Office in Boulder.  The heavy wet snow produced scattered outages across the urban corridor.  Numerous branches snapped and damaged parked vehicles.  Multiple car accidents as well, with many vehicles getting stuck in ditches during the peak period of the heaviest snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snowfall developed in the mountains, heaviest north of the I-70 Corridor where storm totals ranged from 8 to 24 inches.  Snow and blowing snow reduced the visibilities to zero across much of the Interstate 76 and Interstate 70 corridors east of Denver.  Strongest winds gusting from 45 to 65 mph across the plains extended northeast of line from Fort Collins to Limon.  The snow and blowing snow caused widespread blizzard conditions.  Storm totals ranged from 2 to 10 inches, and produced whiteout conditions and snow drifts from 5 to 10 feet in deep.  ||Nearly every road across northeast Colorado was closed or shutdown including the I-76 and I-70 corridors east of Denver to the state-line.  Dozens of school closures were also implemented.  Blowing and drifting snow stranded motorists, with dozens of rescues reported.  At Denver International Airport, 116 flights were cancelled and  approximately 800 flights delayed.  After the snow ended, strong winds and areas of blowing and drifting snow continued through the remainder of the week, which forced some extended road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 18 inches near Rabbit Ears Pass, with 9 inches near Mount Zirkel.

CZ_NAME: PUEBLO VICINITY / PUEBLO COUNTY BELOW 6300 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the area during the evening hours, bringing with it a brief period of snowfall, gusty northerly winds, and frigid temperatures. Snowfall amounts generally stayed light, in the two to four inch range. The main impacts during this period stemmed from the extreme cold, with many locations over the plains observing wind chill values of 25 below zero to 35 below zero. Some locations across El Paso county observed wind chill values of 40 below zero to 50 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill value of 25 below zero. Pueblo airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snowfall developed in the mountains, heaviest north of the I-70 Corridor where storm totals ranged from 8 to 24 inches.  Snow and blowing snow reduced the visibilities to zero across much of the Interstate 76 and Interstate 70 corridors east of Denver.  Strongest winds gusting from 45 to 65 mph across the plains extended northeast of line from Fort Collins to Limon.  The snow and blowing snow caused widespread blizzard conditions.  Storm totals ranged from 2 to 10 inches, and produced whiteout conditions and snow drifts from 5 to 10 feet in deep.  ||Nearly every road across northeast Colorado was closed or shutdown including the I-76 and I-70 corridors east of Denver to the state-line.  Dozens of school closures were also implemented.  Blowing and drifting snow stranded motorists, with dozens of rescues reported.  At Denver International Airport, 116 flights were cancelled and  approximately 800 flights delayed.  After the snow ended, strong winds and areas of blowing and drifting snow continued through the remainder of the week, which forced some extended road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions with whiteout conditions, with peak gusts from 45 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of December 28th, East-Central Colorado saw rainfall turn to snowfall as temperatures cooled behind a cold front that moved through the area. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 inch up to 7 inches within the region. The Colorado Department of Transportation closed highways and roads within the areas seeing snowfall as a safety precaution. The snowfall lasted into the afternoon hours of December 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Yuma County producing total snow accumulations ranging from 1.5 to 6 inches across the county. The highest snow accumulation was reported at 2 miles north-northwest of Kirk, Colorado. At times, the snowfall reduced visibility to around three quarters of a statute mile. U.S. Highway 36 was reported closed from the Kansas/Colorado border to the Yuma/Washington county border due to the snow. Key Impacts: heavy snow, transportation delays, road closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Kit Carson County producing total snow accumulations ranging from 4 to 7 inches across the county. The highest snow accumulation was reported at 13 miles southwest of Joes, Colorado. At times, the snowfall reduced visibility to around a half of a statute mile. Interstate 70 was reported closed from the Kansas/Colorado border to the Kit Carson/Lincoln county border due to the snow. Key Impacts: heavy snow, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust storm was reported north-northeast of Kit Carson as an arctic cold front moved through.  Behind the arctic air mass, wind gusts of 20-35 MPH gusts and temperatures falling to the teens below zero, moved over East Central Colorado.  The coldest wind chills approached -50F during the worst of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter mile visibilities were reported briefly from blowing dust 10 NNE of Kit Carson as the arctic front moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the area during the evening hours, bringing with it a brief period of snowfall, gusty northerly winds, and frigid temperatures. Snowfall amounts generally stayed light, in the two to four inch range. The main impacts during this period stemmed from the extreme cold, with many locations over the plains observing wind chill values of 25 below zero to 35 below zero. Some locations across El Paso county observed wind chill values of 40 below zero to 50 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill value of 33 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the latter half of the morning on December 2nd, strong, dry southwesterly winds began picking up dirt and dust in Eastern Colorado and Western Kansas. This led to areas of reduced visibility due to the blowing dust across Eastern Colorado, Western Kansas, and extreme Southwest Nebraska. A strong cold front entered the East Central Colorado around 11 AM and quickly moved southeast. Before this cold front entered into the Tri-State area, it was already collecting dust along its leading edge, forming a wall of dust resembling a haboob. As this cold front swept through the area, it gathered more dust and grew in severity and longevity. Many photos and videos showing a wall of dust a few hundred feet high were sent to the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office in Goodland, Kansas. When the wall of dust moved over an area, visibility would drop to near or less than 0.25 mile with wind gusts near 70 mile per hour. Strong northwesterly winds and 0.25-mile visibility behind the initial wall of dust would continue for an hour or longer before subsiding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust 1 mile west of Joes, Colorado of 59 knots. There were numerous other reports of winds above 50 kts in Yuma county during this event, but the 59 knot gust was the highest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving but powerful cold front pushed across western Colorado and brought moderate snow to the mountains and some adjacent valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with winds exceeding 45 mph in the mountains at times, resulting in reduced visibilities. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of western Colorado. Wind gusts exceeded 50 mph with a snow squall that impacted portions of Rio Blanco and Routt counties as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

Wind gusts exceeded 50 mph with a snow squall that impacted portions of Rio Blanco and Routt counties as visibilities dropped to less than one quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted in excess of 60 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snowfall developed in the mountains, heaviest north of the I-70 Corridor where storm totals ranged from 8 to 24 inches.  Snow and blowing snow reduced the visibilities to zero across much of the Interstate 76 and Interstate 70 corridors east of Denver.  Strongest winds gusting from 45 to 65 mph across the plains extended northeast of line from Fort Collins to Limon.  The snow and blowing snow caused widespread blizzard conditions.  Storm totals ranged from 2 to 10 inches, and produced whiteout conditions and snow drifts from 5 to 10 feet in deep.  ||Nearly every road across northeast Colorado was closed or shutdown including the I-76 and I-70 corridors east of Denver to the state-line.  Dozens of school closures were also implemented.  Blowing and drifting snow stranded motorists, with dozens of rescues reported.  At Denver International Airport, 116 flights were cancelled and  approximately 800 flights delayed.  After the snow ended, strong winds and areas of blowing and drifting snow continued through the remainder of the week, which forced some extended road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds produced areas of blowing and drifting snow through the rest of the week.  As a result, several roadways remained closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive arctic air mass accompanied a strong cold front that dropped across western Colorado. The lift from the front itself paired with strong jet support aloft and minor moisture resulted in scattered showers across the Continental Divide. While snowfall totals weren't overly impressive with this system, generally light to moderate, the combination of snowfall with wind gusts in excess of 70 mph at times in the higher terrain resulted in blizzard-like conditions and significant travel impacts.||In the wake of the system, the arctic air overhead paired with continued breezy conditions resulted in bitterly cold wind chills for the northern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times which resulted in significantly reduced visibility in blowing snow and hazardous travel. Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. The cold front brought wind chills down to -34 degrees F at the automated station at Dead Horse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast over East-Central Colorado generating nearly eight hours of blizzard conditions across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties. Snow amounts varied from two to eight inches across the two counties. The greatest snow accumulations were along Highway 34 near the city of Yuma, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system generated roughly 7.5 hours of blizzard conditions across Kit Carson County. Winds were out of the northwest with gusts up to 62 mph recorded. The strongest gust was observed via the Kit Carson County Airport ASOS station in Burlington, Colorado. The high winds were observed from 10:18 AM MT through 12:45 PM MT. The only damage reported from the high winds was a broken window in Flagler, Colorado. Visibility reductions ranged as low as one-quarter mile to near zero across the county. Snow amounts ranged from one to three inches. Key impacts: blowing snow, snow packed roadways, and blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast over East-Central Colorado generating nearly eight hours of blizzard conditions across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties. Snow amounts varied from two to eight inches across the two counties. The greatest snow accumulations were along Highway 34 near the city of Yuma, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast over East-Central Colorado generating seven hours of blizzard conditions across Yuma County. There were numerous reports from the public, along with the Wray and Yuma AWOS stations, of visibilities dropping to between a quarter mile to zero miles. Winds were out of the west-northwest with gusts up to 48 mph. During the event, up to eight inches of snow fell with drifts reportedly up to seven feet tall in spots. Key impacts: blowing snow, snow packed roadways, heavy snow, and blizzard conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast over East-Central Colorado generating heavy blowing snow across Yuma County. There were numerous reports from the public, along with the Wray and Yuma AWOS stations, of visibilities dropping to between a quarter mile to zero miles during blizzard conditions. During the event, snow amounts ranged from a trace to eight inches across the county with drifts reportedly up to seven feet tall in spots. The greatest snow accumulations occurred along and north of Highway 34 near the city of Yuma. Key impacts: blowing snow, snow packed roadways, heavy snow, and blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of December 14th, high winds were observed in East-Central Colorado behind a departing winter weather system that moved towards the Nebraska/South Dakota/Iowa border. The highest non-thunderstorm wind gust recorded in this region was 60 mph. These high winds allowed for blowing snow that reduced visibilities down to a reported 1.75 statute miles. While there were hazardous traveling conditions within the region as a result, no injuries or damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KITR ASOS reported non-thunderstorm wind gusts over 58 mph during this time with a maximum wind gust of 60 mph at 2:39 PM MST.
